content.text: Lists are one of the most frequently used HTML constructs. They group related items, establish sequence, and give assistive technologies the semantic context needed to announce content correctly. HTML provides three distinct list types, each suited to a different kind of relationship between items.
(description list — term + definition pairs). All three are block-level elements.

attrs.title: HTML List Elements

with a bullet marker (disc by default). The only permitted direct children of

<dl>
  <dt>Component</dt>
  <dd>Controls a patch of the UI and is defined with a class and @Component decorator.</dd>

  <dt>Service</dt>
  <dd>Encapsulates logic not tied to a view and is injected via the DI system.</dd>
  <dd>Typically marked with @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }).</dd>

  <dt>Module</dt>
  <dd>Legacy grouping of components, directives, and providers.</dd>
</dl>

Browsers automatically change the bullet style at each nesting level (disc → circle → square for
). This browser default is overridden by most CSS resets, so in a real project you will control nesting styles explicitly.
The three CSS properties that control list markers are

block (Angular 17+) is the standard way to render a dynamic list. It replaces the older
structural directive. Both produce the same DOM, but
expression, which makes identity-tracking explicit and intentional.

When Angular re-renders a list after data changes, it needs to know which DOM nodes correspond to which data items. Without tracking, it tears down and rebuilds every
on every change detection cycle — destroying focus state, animations, and component instances. A stable
property) lets Angular reuse DOM nodes for unchanged items and only update the ones that changed.
with Angular, you almost never need to display the index explicitly — the browser numbers the
implicit variable only when you need it for logic (e.g. alternating styles), not for display:

@for (step of steps; track step.id; let i = $index) {
  1. {{ step.title }}
    2. }

@for (entry of metadata; track entry.key) {
{{ entry.label }}
{{ entry.value }}
}

Screen readers announce list semantics: "list, 3 items" before reading the first item, and "end of list" afterward. This helps users understand the content structure before engaging with it. A few nuances:
When you remove the visual bullet with
, Safari's VoiceOver stops announcing the element as a list. If the list semantics matter to screen reader users (e.g. a navigation menu, a feature list), add
element are commonly wrapped in a
. The list provides structure when CSS is disabled and gives AT users the item count. Add
if markers are hidden via CSS.
For interactive lists (e.g. a listbox, a combobox dropdown), the native
does not provide arrow-key navigation. Use
with ARIA and keyboard event handlers, or reach for Angular CDK's
support in screen readers is uneven. JAWS and NVDA announce dl structure well; VoiceOver is inconsistent. For glossary-like UIs that must be reliably accessible, consider a
with scope attributes as an alternative.
wrappers for visual rows instead of lists.
elements has no list semantics. Screen readers won't announce an item count. If the items form a logical collection, use a proper list element.
elements placed directly inside a list container are invalid HTML and create unpredictable browser behaviour.
Numbered lists imply that the order matters. A list of team members or product categories should be
even if they happen to be sorted alphabetically.
mandatory (the compiler errors if it is missing). For lists without a stable unique key, use
as a last resort — but be aware that tracking by index re-uses DOM nodes by position, not identity, which breaks animations and focus when items are reordered.

When to use each list type
Feature list, tag cloud, nav links

A common real-world pattern is a list whose visible items change based on user input — a search box narrows the set, a filter button toggles a category. The following standalone Angular component demonstrates this end-to-end:
Several details are worth noting in this component:
derives the filtered list lazily. It only recalculates when
changes — there is no manual subscription or OnInit hook needed.
tells screen readers to announce updates to the list after the user stops typing — but not mid-keystroke.
when the filtered set is empty, keeping the list container always present for AT.
tracks by a stable numeric ID. When the filter narrows the list from 6 → 2 items, Angular removes the 4 unmatched
elements rather than re-creating the remaining 2.
elements creates the same number of DOM nodes, all of which participate in layout and paint. Three strategies control this cost:
Show a fixed page of items (e.g. 20 per page) and render only that slice. Straightforward and works for any list type. The
signal that updates when the page changes.
renders only the visible items plus a small buffer, recycling DOM nodes as the user scrolls. It requires every item to have a known or estimated height and replaces the plain
wrapper, so it is a structural change — plan for it early if the list can grow large.
so Angular skips checking unchanged items during every change-detection cycle. Pair this with stable
to keep DOM nodes alive across data refreshes.

under ~200 items, render them all and trust the browser. Between 200–2000 items, consider pagination. Above 2000 items, virtual scrolling usually pays for its added complexity.
Unit tests for dynamic lists verify two things: that the correct number of items renders, and that the content of each item matches the data. Angular's
after mutating component state to flush the update and repaint the DOM.

to assert the correct HTML element type.
rendered is the fallback — so the count assertion is still 1, not 0.
counts the items from the ARIA perspective rather than the raw DOM.
Every browser ships list styles that are reset or overridden in production Angular projects. Understanding the defaults prevents surprises when working outside a design system.
do not include a CSS reset by default. The styles Angular injects into the head reset little beyond

. If you are using Tailwind CSS (which includes Preflight) or another design system that provides a reset, list defaults will be stripped. Always check which baseline your project uses before debugging unexpected list indentation or missing markers.
